title: feat: 为高风险 PR 增加有界 GitHub Codex Review 门禁
author: yangsf5
body:
## 背景

gstack 当前在 `/ship` 和 `/land-and-deploy` 中会读取本地 Review 记录与 CI 状态，但没有可靠地等待 GitHub 上 `chatgpt-codex-connector` 的云端 PR Review。Codex 后台的自动或智能触发没有公开、稳定的“已开始处理”信号，实际可能在 PR 已合并后才产出意见，也可能根本没有触发，导致工作流要么过早合并，要么无限等待。

本改动面向希望把 GitHub Codex Review 作为额外防线、同时控制额度消耗的 gstack 用户。默认不触发云端 Review；用户显式启用后，gstack 只在高风险 PR 上发送 `@codex review`，并以有限状态机跟踪当前 HEAD 的结果。

## 当前状态

- `land-and-deploy/SKILL.md.tmpl:380` 附近的合并前门禁只检查本地 gstack Review 记录、测试、文档和 PR 描述，没有 GitHub Codex Review 状态。
- `bin/gstack-config:89` 现有 `codex_reviews` 只控制本地跨模型 Review，不应复用为 GitHub Connector 开关，否则会混淆两种额度与执行位置。
- 自动触发是否启动无法从 PR API 可靠判断；显式评论 `@codex review` 后可以用机器人对该评论的 👀 reaction 作为 ACK，并用绑定当前 `headRefOid` 的 Review 或 👍 reaction 判断完成。

## 建议变更

新增一个默认关闭、可配置的 GitHub Codex Review 合并前门禁：

```text
OFF / LOW_RISK ───────────────────────────────> SKIPPED
HIGH_RISK -> REQUESTED -> ACKNOWLEDGED -> COMPLETED
                    └-> ACK_TIMEOUT
                                 └-> COMPLETION_TIMEOUT
```

- `github_codex_review: off | risk | always`，默认 `off`，避免升级后意外消耗用户额度。
- `risk` 模式由可测试的确定性规则判断。以下任一条件命中即触发：变更文件数 `>= 10`；新增与删除总行数 `>= 200`；路径匹配 `**/{auth,security,permissions,secrets,migrations,migrate,schema,database,db}/**`、`.github/workflows/**`、`**/{deploy,infra,infrastructure,terraform,k8s,kubernetes}/**`。只有当全部文件都属于 `docs/**`、`README*`、`CHANGELOG*`、`VERSION`、`.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/**` 时才视为文档/版本专用并跳过。`always` 对任何非空 PR 都触发，`off` 永不触发。
- 对当前 PR HEAD 使用带隐藏 marker 的 `@codex review` 评论；同一 HEAD 已请求或已有完成结果时不得重复评论。
- 默认最多等待 120 秒取得 ACK；ACK 后默认最多再等待 600 秒取得完成结果；实际使用配置值。每 5 秒轮询一次，网络瞬断采用最多 3 次指数退避后继续计入总超时时间。超时只降级为警告，继续依赖 gstack 本地 `/review`、测试和 CI，不能无限等待。
- 只有与当前 HEAD 一致的云端 Review 才算完成。Review 后又 push 时，旧结果标为 stale，按模式重新判断并最多为新 HEAD 请求一次。
- 当前 HEAD 存在未解决的 Codex Review thread 时阻止合并，报告 thread URL；修复或明确解决后重新运行 `/land-and-deploy`。
- GitHub Codex App 未安装、未授权或 API 不可用时，输出明确状态与恢复建议，但不把第三方可用性升级为硬阻塞。

### 实现细节

1. 新增 `bin/gstack-github-codex-review`：
   - 使用 `gh pr view` / `gh api` 获取 PR number、HEAD、Review、reaction 与 review thread。
   - 提供 `assess`、`status`、`run` 三个子命令：`assess` 只做风险判断；`status` 只读当前 GitHub 状态；`run` 完成判断、幂等请求和有界等待。公共参数为 `--mode`、`--pr`，`run` 另接受 `--ack-timeout`、`--completion-timeout`、`--poll-interval`。
   - stdout 只输出单个 JSON 文档，进度写入 stderr。JSON 固定包含 `schema_version`、`status`、`blocking`、`reason`、`mode`、`pr`、`request`、`timeout_stage`、`unresolved_threads`、`warnings`。状态值仅为 `SKIPPED`、`REQUESTED`、`ACKNOWLEDGED`、`COMPLETED`、`TIMEOUT`、`BLOCKED`、`ERROR`。
   - 退出码：`0` 表示可继续（包括 `TIMEOUT` 与第三方 `ERROR` 降级）；`2` 表示存在未解决 thread、必须阻止合并；`64` 表示参数或配置错误；其他非零值仅保留给 helper 自身不可恢复错误。
   - 请求评论包含 `<!-- gstack-github-codex-review head=<sha> -->`，幂等键为 PR + 完整 HEAD SHA。
   - 机器人账号匹配限制为 `chatgpt-codex-connector` 及其 GitHub Bot 形式，不能把普通用户 reaction 当作 ACK 或完成。
   - 👀 和 👍 都必须来自目标机器人、且位于包含当前 HEAD marker 的同一条请求评论。👀 表示 ACK；👍 表示该 HEAD 无建议并完成。reaction 被删除后，以 API 当前状态为准；已有绑定当前 HEAD 的正式 Review 仍优先作为完成证据。
   - 正式 Review 仅当 `user.login` 为目标机器人且 `commit_id` 等于当前 HEAD 时有效。GraphQL `reviewThreads` 中首条评论来自目标机器人、其 Review commit 等于当前 HEAD、并且 `isResolved=false`、`isOutdated=false` 的 thread 才是 blocker。
   - GitHub 未认证、App 未安装、限流或 GraphQL/API 在 3 次重试后失败时返回非阻塞 `ERROR` 并写入 `warnings`；不能伪装成 `COMPLETED`。若已知有当前 HEAD Review 但无法读取 thread，readiness report 必须显示显著警告。
2. 扩展 `bin/gstack-config`：
   - 新增三个有严格值域的配置项：`github_codex_review`、`github_codex_ack_timeout_seconds`、`github_codex_completion_timeout_seconds`。
   - 默认分别为 `off`、`120`、`600`；超时必须是正整数。
3. 在 `land-and-deploy/SKILL.md.tmpl` 的合并前 readiness gate 中调用 helper：
   - 在最终 readiness report 中显示 `SKIPPED / REQUESTED / ACKNOWLEDGED / COMPLETED / TIMEOUT / BLOCKED`。
   - `BLOCKED` 增加 blocker；`TIMEOUT` 增加 warning；`SKIPPED` 不增加 warning。
   - 修改模板后运行技能生成器，更新 `land-and-deploy/SKILL.md` 以及 `test/fixtures/golden/{claude,codex,factory}-land-and-deploy-SKILL.md`；不直接手改生成文件。
4. 为 helper、配置验证、模板生成一致性和关键门禁文案增加自动化测试。

## 验收标准

1. 新安装或未配置用户运行 `/land-and-deploy` 时不会发送 `@codex review`。
2. `risk` 模式对文档专用 PR 返回 `SKIPPED`，对规模阈值或敏感路径命中的 PR 返回需要 Review，并给出机器可读原因。
3. 同一 PR、同一 HEAD 连续运行两次，GitHub 上最多只有一条带对应 marker 的请求评论。
4. 只有目标机器人在请求评论上的 👀 reaction 才把状态推进到 `ACKNOWLEDGED`。
5. 只有绑定当前 HEAD 的目标机器人 Review，或目标机器人在请求评论上的 👍 reaction，才把状态推进到 `COMPLETED`。
6. 使用 1 秒测试配置时，1 秒无 ACK 或 ACK 后 1 秒无完成结果均返回 `TIMEOUT`，`timeout_stage` 分别为 `ack`、`completion`，且不继续轮询；默认配置值另断言为 120/600 秒。
7. 当前 HEAD 有未解决 Codex Review thread 时 readiness report 出现 blocker，且 `/land-and-deploy` 不执行 merge。
8. push 新提交后旧 HEAD 的 Review 不计为当前完成；新 HEAD 可以创建且只创建一次新请求。
9. `github_codex_review` 的非法值与非正整数超时配置被拒绝，并保留原配置。
10. mock `gh` 覆盖未认证、限流和 GraphQL 失败时返回非阻塞 `ERROR`，不误报 `COMPLETED`，也不无限重试。
11. `bun test`、`bun run gen:skill-docs --host codex` 与完整技能生成检查通过；现有 `land-and-deploy`、配置和生成器测试零新增失败。

## 测试计划

| 层级 | 内容 | 数量 |
|------|------|------|
| 单元 | 风险分类、Bot 身份匹配、状态转换、当前 HEAD、超时和配置值域 | +12 以上 |
| 集成 | 使用 mock `gh` 覆盖幂等请求、ACK、完成、stale HEAD、未解决 thread | +8 以上 |
| 生成检查 | 模板生成到 Claude/Codex/Factory 后都包含门禁且不漂移 | +3 以上 |

不增加真实 GitHub Codex Review 的付费 E2E；真实连接器只在用户本机启用后由实际 PR 验证。

## 回滚计划

- 用户级回滚：`gstack-config set github_codex_review off`，立即恢复为仅使用现有本地 Review、测试与 CI。
- 代码级回滚：回滚本 PR；不涉及数据库、线上数据或不可逆迁移。
- 请求评论是普通 GitHub 评论，无需删除；它包含 HEAD marker，仅用于审计与幂等判断。

## 工作量估算

- helper 与状态机：4 小时
- gstack 配置与模板接入：2 小时
- 单元/集成/生成测试：3 小时
- 文档、Review 与发布验证：1 小时

## 文件参考

| 文件 | 修改 |
|------|------|
| `bin/gstack-github-codex-review` | 新增风险判断、幂等请求、状态读取和有界等待 helper |
| `bin/gstack-config` | 新增 GitHub Codex Review 模式和超时配置 |
| `land-and-deploy/SKILL.md.tmpl` | 在 readiness gate 接入云端 Review 状态 |
| `land-and-deploy/SKILL.md` | 由生成器更新 |
| `test/fixtures/golden/{claude,codex,factory}-land-and-deploy-SKILL.md` | 由生成器更新多 host 金丝雀 |
| `test/gstack-github-codex-review.test.ts` | helper 单元与 mock `gh` 集成测试 |
| `test/skill-validation.test.ts` | 配置与门禁文案回归检查 |

## 不在范围内

- 不处理历史 PR 中尚未解决的 Codex Review 评论。
- 不修改 GitHub Codex App 本身，也不尝试推断后台“智能触发”是否启动。
- 不自动修复 Review 建议；本期只提供可靠触发、检测、当前 HEAD 约束和合并阻断。
- 不替代 gstack 本地 `/review`、测试、CI 或人工判断。
- 不在默认配置下产生额外 Codex 云端额度消耗。

## 相关事项

- #1036 — 本地 Codex 调用稳定性问题；本功能使用 GitHub Connector，执行路径不同，但同样采用有界超时和降级策略。

title: bug: skill:check fails because claude/SKILL.md is not generated
author: GoloCatMash
body:
### Reproduction

Environment: origin/main 83238f9ba28b9a5dfa3405fa625b70f7b5b99d39 plus an unrelated resolver-only commit.

Run:

    bun run gen:skill-docs --host codex --dry-run
    bun run skill:check

All Codex skills are present and all host freshness checks report fresh, but skill:check exits 1 with:

    claude/SKILL.md — generated file missing! Run: bun run gen:skill-docs

The repository contains the corresponding template, while the expected generated file is absent.

### Expected

Either the normal generation path should produce claude/SKILL.md, or skill:check should not require that target.

title: test: gbrain-missing PATH case leaks host /opt/homebrew/bin
author: GoloCatMash
body:
### Reproduction

Environment: macOS arm64 with gbrain installed in /opt/homebrew/bin, origin/main 83238f9ba28b9a5dfa3405fa625b70f7b5b99d39.

Run:

    bun test test/gstack-gbrain-source-wireup.test.ts

The test named "--strict + gbrain missing on PATH: exits 2" fails with expected 2, received 1.

The fixture says its fake bin is empty but constructs PATH with /opt/homebrew/bin. On a developer host where gbrain is installed there, the supposedly missing executable is visible and the test is not hermetic.

### Expected

The missing-PATH fixture should exclude host package-manager directories or otherwise shadow gbrain deterministically.

title: bug: browse stop exits 1 and retries after successful shutdown
author: GoloCatMash
body:
### Reproduction

Environment: macOS arm64, origin/main 83238f9ba28b9a5dfa3405fa625b70f7b5b99d39.

1. Run the generated browse binary with status. It starts and reports healthy.
2. Run the same binary with stop.

Observed output:

    [browse] Server connection lost. Restarting...
    [browse] [browse] Server crashed twice in a row — aborting

The stop command exits 1, although the daemon is actually gone afterward and no matching process remains.

### Expected

An intentional stop should not enter the crash-restart path. It should stop the daemon and exit 0.

title: setup-gbrain calls bin/gstack-memory-ingest & bin/gstack-gbrain-sync without .ts extension → "script missing at install root" on every host
author: inside-ziwu
body:
## Summary

`/setup-gbrain`'s transcript-ingest steps call `bin/gstack-memory-ingest` and `bin/gstack-gbrain-sync` **without the `.ts` extension**. Neither bare name exists in `bin/` — only `gstack-memory-ingest.ts` and `gstack-gbrain-sync.ts` ship. The agent dutifully reports the script as missing from the gstack install root and the ingest/full-sync steps dead-end. Both files are also mode `644` (no `+x`), so even a correctly-spelled direct exec would fail without `bun run`.

Hit this live running `/gstack-setup-gbrain` under Codex; the Claude render has the same references, so every host is affected.

## Affected call sites

`setup-gbrain/SKILL.md.tmpl` (canonical source, confirmed present on `main` today):

- Line 690: `~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-memory-ingest --probe`
- Line 735: `~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-gbrain-sync --full --no-brain-sync`

Rendered copies inherit it: Claude render `setup-gbrain/SKILL.md:1444,1489`; Codex render `gstack-setup-gbrain/SKILL.md:1432,1477` (as `$GSTACK_ROOT/bin/...`).

## Repro

```bash
ls bin/ | grep -E 'memory-ingest|gbrain-sync'
# gstack-gbrain-sync.ts
# gstack-memory-ingest.ts        ← no bare names

grep -n 'bin/gstack-memory-ingest[^.]\|bin/gstack-gbrain-sync[^.]' setup-gbrain/SKILL.md.tmpl
# 690: ... bin/gstack-memory-ingest --probe
# 735: ... bin/gstack-gbrain-sync --full --no-brain-sync

~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-memory-ingest --probe
# no such file or directory
```

## Expected

The repo already has the correct pattern one skill over — `sync-gbrain/SKILL.md` invokes the same orchestrator as:

```bash
bun run ~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-gbrain-sync.ts <user-args>
```

## Suggested fix (either works)

1. Change both call sites to `bun run .../gstack-memory-ingest.ts --probe` / `bun run .../gstack-gbrain-sync.ts --full --no-brain-sync`, matching `sync-gbrain`.
2. Or ship bare-name `+x` wrappers in `bin/` like the rest of the `gstack-*` scripts, so skill docs never need to care which scripts are TS.

Option 1 is the one-line-diff. Note both files' shebang is `#!/usr/bin/env bun`, so any fix that relies on direct exec still needs `bun` on PATH in non-interactive host shells (mise/asdf-managed bun installs won't always be there) — `bun run` from the skill text sidesteps nothing there, but it's worth keeping the invocation explicit rather than shebang-dependent.

## Environment

- gstack v1.60.1.0 (clean reinstall, `~/.codex/skills/gstack/bin` → repo `bin/` symlink), bug also confirmed on `main`
- macOS (darwin 25.5), hosts: Codex CLI 0.144.x and Claude Code renders

title: Headed mode broken on macOS: rebrand invalidates the Chrome-for-Testing bundle signature (GPU exit_code=5), and the parent-PID watchdog kills handoff sessions
author: abhij9750
body:
## Summary

Two independent bugs make headed mode unusable on macOS (arm64, gstack v1.58.5.0). Both have a clear root cause and a small fix. This is *not* #1781 (busy-vs-dead daemon / SingletonLock) — that fix is in the tree and working; these are separate.

Symptoms, in the order a user hits them:

1. `$B handoff` never opens a window: `GPU process exited unexpectedly: exit_code=5`, preceded by a bogus crashpad warning (`in_range_cast.h:38 value -634136515 out of range`).
2. `$B stop` prints `[browse] Server crashed twice in a row — aborting` and leaves a *healthy* daemon behind.
3. Retrying handoff crash-loops the daemon.

---

## Bug 1 — the rebrand corrupts the Chrome-for-Testing bundle (headed dead)

`browse/src/browser-manager.ts` (~L503-540, in `launchHeaded()`) rebrands the browser for the macOS menu bar:

```ts
const patched = plistContent.replace(/Google Chrome for Testing/g, 'GStack Browser');
fs.writeFileSync(chromePlist, patched);
```

That regex is unscoped, so it also rewrites **`CFBundleExecutable`** — which then names a binary that does not exist (the file in `Contents/MacOS/` is still `Google Chrome for Testing`). `fs.copyFileSync(iconSrc, destIcon)` a few lines later overwrites the signed `Contents/Resources/app.icns` as well.

Result on disk:

```
Contents/Info.plist:  CFBundleExecutable  = GStack Browser   ← no such file
Contents/MacOS/    :  "Google Chrome for Testing"            ← the only binary
$ spctl -a -vv ".../Google Chrome for Testing.app"
  → rejected (source=no usable signature)
```

Chromium's macOS launcher needs the outer bundle to validate before it can spawn the GPU/renderer helper apps, so the GPU process dies at launch (`exit_code=5`) and crashpad can't attach (hence the garbage-handle warning — there is no real crash report; `~/Library/Logs/DiagnosticReports/` is empty).

Headless is unaffected because `chrome-headless-shell` is a bare Mach-O: no `.app`, no plist, no helper processes. That's why the failure looks so mode-specific.

Worth noting the blast radius: the patch lands in the **shared** `~/Library/Caches/ms-playwright/chromium-<rev>` bundle, so it breaks that revision for every other Playwright consumer on the machine, not just gstack. It is also persistent — once written, every later launch is broken, including after a gstack reinstall.

**Fix:** don't mutate a signed bundle in the shared Playwright cache. Either drop the rebrand, or copy the `.app` to a gstack-owned directory, patch only `CFBundleName`/`CFBundleDisplayName`, and re-sign (`codesign -f -s -`). Locally I dropped it — a cosmetic window title isn't worth a browser that won't start.

## Bug 2 — the parent-PID watchdog kills headed daemons started via `handoff`

- `browse/src/cli.ts` (~L315) defaults `BROWSE_PARENT_PID` to the CLI's own PID → watchdog **on**.
- `browse/src/server.ts` (~L706): when the parent dies, shut down if `headed || tunnelActive`.

The `connect` path sidesteps this by setting `BROWSE_PARENT_PID=0` (cli.ts ~L1093, ~L1334). **`handoff` does not** — it flips an already-running headless daemon to headed in place. Under an agent harness (Claude Code) each command runs in its own short-lived shell, so the recorded parent is already dead. ~15s after handoff the watchdog fires, sees `headed`, and shuts down the window it just handed to the user. Next command → ECONNREFUSED → restart → handoff → killed again → `Server crashed twice in a row — aborting`.

The `headed` half of that condition is effectively dead code for the `connect` path (watchdog already off there), so it only ever fires in the case where it does harm.

**Fix:** shut down on parent death for `tunnelActive` only. A headed window is a human's session; it should end on browser disconnect, `$B disconnect`, or idle timeout — not because a shell exited.

## Bug 3 (small) — `$B stop` restarts the server it just stopped

In `sendCommand`'s connection-error branch (`cli.ts`, ~L602): the daemon closes its socket as it exits, the CLI reads `ECONNRESET` as a crash, kills + restarts the daemon, re-sends `stop`, resets again, and finally reports `Server crashed twice in a row`. Net effect: `$B stop` prints a scary error and leaves a healthy server running.

**Fix:** a connection reset while the command *is* `stop` means the stop succeeded. Return success instead of entering the restart path.

## Also: `bun` resolved from PATH only

`cli.ts` (~L341) spawns the daemon with `nodeSpawn('bun', ...)`. The default install (`~/.bun/bin`) isn't on PATH in non-login shells (again: agent harnesses). The spawn ENOENTs with `stdio: 'ignore'`, so it's silent, and surfaces 8 seconds later as the misleading `Server failed to start within 8s`. Falling back to `$HOME/.bun/bin/bun` when bare `bun` doesn't resolve removes a whole class of confusing startup failures.

## Environment

macOS 15 (Darwin 24.5.0), arm64. gstack v1.58.5.0. playwright-core 1.58.2, Chrome for Testing 149.0.7827.55 (chromium rev 1228).

## Verification after patching locally

- Bundle stays valid after a headed launch (`CFBundleExecutable` intact, `app.icns` untouched).
- `$B handoff` opens a visible window; `$B status` → `Mode: headed`; still alive 80s later (previously dead in ~15s).
- Headed browser drives fine (`goto`, `js`, `snapshot -i`).
- `$B stop` stops the daemon, is idempotent, and prints no error.
- `$B disconnect` leaves zero orphan `Google Chrome for Testing` processes.
- `bun test` failure count unchanged vs pristine source (8, all pre-existing: gbrain-detect, user-slug, Swift/XCTest).

Happy to send a PR for any/all of these if the direction looks right — particularly whether you want the rebrand dropped or moved to a copied+resigned bundle.

title: Audit findings: duplicated ~120-line skill boilerplate (51/56), over-prescriptive STRICT recipes, hardcoded 'Claude Opus 4.7' commit trailer
author: lemonbalms
body:
While auditing my local instruction files against Anthropic's Fable 5 prompting guidance, I ran a pass over the gstack skill suite (v1.60.1.0 as installed). Three findings, bundled here since they're all low-urgency maintenance items — happy to split them into separate issues if you prefer.

---

## Issue 1: Duplicated ~120-line boilerplate block across 51/56 skills

**Title:** 51/56 skills embed an identical ~120-line AskUserQuestion/Conductor/plan-tune boilerplate block

**Summary:**
An audit of the gstack skill suite found that 51 of 56 skill files contain a near-identical
~120-line boilerplate block covering AskUserQuestion setup, Conductor wiring, and plan-tune
integration. The duplication inflates every skill file and means any fix or update to this
shared logic has to be hand-propagated across 51 files.

**Details:**
- Example instance: `review/SKILL.md:335-457`.
- The block recurs near-verbatim in the remaining 50 skills that include it.
- Total duplicated content is roughly 51 × 120 = ~6,100 lines of repeated text across the suite.

**Suggested fix:**
Extract the shared block to a single include file (e.g. `_shared/askuserquestion-conductor.md`)
and have each skill reference it, or trim the boilerplate to the minimum each skill actually
needs. Either approach removes the multi-file edit burden and shrinks per-skill file size.

---

## Issue 2: Heavy STRICT step recipes degrade output on newer models

**Title:** Prescriptive STRICT phase/step recipes conflict with Anthropic's Fable 5 prompting guidance

**Summary:**
Several core skills (`spec`, `ship`, `autoplan`) use heavily prescriptive "STRICT" process
blocks with large numbers of MUST/step markers. Anthropic's guidance for Fable 5 notes that
instructions written for older, less capable models are "often too prescriptive ... and can
degrade output quality" on newer models — the STRICT recipes in this suite are exactly that
pattern.

**Details:**
- `spec/SKILL.md:847`: `Process (STRICT — do not skip or combine phases)`, followed by 92
  MUST markers and 96 step markers within that skill alone.
- `ship/SKILL.md`: 71 MUST markers, 99 step markers.
- `autoplan/SKILL.md`: 67 MUST markers, 100 step markers.
- Suite-wide: 84 STRICT markers across the skill set.

**Suggested fix:**
Rewrite the STRICT recipes as intent + boundary statements (what must be true at the end,
what must not happen) rather than enumerated MUST/step sequences, per Anthropic's current
prompting guidance for capable models. This should reduce both token overhead and the
degradation risk the guidance describes.

---

## Issue 3: Hardcoded model name in commit trailers

**Title:** Commit trailers hardcode `Claude Opus 4.7`, will drift as models change

**Summary:**
Two skills generate git commit trailers with a hardcoded model name (`Claude Opus 4.7`)
instead of reflecting whichever model is actually active in the session. This trailer will
silently become inaccurate the moment a session runs on a different model.

**Details:**
- `document-generate/SKILL.md:1197`: `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7`
- `ship/SKILL.md:1232`: `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7`

**Suggested fix:**
Parameterize the trailer to the active model identity (if available to the skill at run time),
or fall back to a version-agnostic `Co-Authored-By: Claude` so the trailer doesn't need to be
updated by hand every time the underlying model changes.

title: browse: daemon's SIGTERM "ignore" is cosmetic — Playwright's default handleSIGTERM closes Chromium anyway
author: DavidCnoops
body:
## Environment
- gstack: 1.58.4.0 (`9fd03fa`)
- OS: macOS 26.3.1 (arm64), Bun 1.3.9
- Playwright: chromium_headless_shell-1208

## Symptom
The daemon advertises that it ignores SIGTERM:

```
[browse] Received SIGTERM (ignoring — use /stop or Ctrl+C for intentional shutdown)
```

…but ~1s after receiving SIGTERM it loses its browser and exits anyway:

```
[browse] Received SIGTERM (ignoring — use /stop or Ctrl+C for intentional shutdown)
[browse] Tab closed (id=1, remaining=0)
[browse] FATAL unhandled rejection: browser?.process is not a function. (...)
```

Captured 3× on macOS with daemon stderr redirected to a file (normal spawns are `stdio: 'ignore'`, so this is invisible in the field).

## Root cause
`chromium.launch()` at `browse/src/browser-manager.ts:372` does not set the `handleSIGINT` / `handleSIGTERM` / `handleSIGHUP` launch options. Playwright's defaults are **true**, so Playwright installs its own process-level signal listeners that close the launched browser when the daemon process receives the signal — completely independent of the daemon's own "ignore" handler in `server.ts`.

Net effect: the app-level SIGTERM handler runs and logs "ignoring", Playwright's handler closes Chromium anyway, the tab closes, `browser.disconnected` fires, and `handleChromiumDisconnect` exits the process (currently via the `browser?.process()` TypeError — #2085 secondary — but it would `process.exit()` by design even without that bug). The "ignore" is cosmetic.

This matters in practice because `killServer()` (`browse/src/cli.ts:157`) leads with SIGTERM: any CLI-initiated restart tears down Chromium through Playwright's hook before the SIGKILL fallback ever fires, so the intended graceful-shutdown/ignore semantics never get a chance to apply. It also means any environment that sprays SIGTERM (session teardown, supervisors) kills the browser even though the daemon deliberately opted out.

## Repro
1. `BROWSE_PARENT_PID=0 bun run browse/src/server.ts 2>&1 | tee /tmp/browse.log`, wait for healthy, `browse goto about:blank`.
2. `kill -TERM <daemon pid>`.
3. Log shows "ignoring", then the tab closes and the daemon exits ~1–2s later.

## Suggested fix
Pass `handleSIGINT: false, handleSIGTERM: false, handleSIGHUP: false` to `chromium.launch()` (and the `launchPersistentContext` call at `browse/src/browser-manager.ts:579`), and let the daemon's own signal handlers own the shutdown policy — they already exist and already encode the intended behavior.

Related: the kill-happy CLI path that sends these SIGTERMs during normal QA is filed separately as #2219.

title: browse: CLI kills a live-but-busy daemon during heavy page loads — ~7s health-probe window too short (follow-up to #1781); all session state lost
author: DavidCnoops
body:
## Environment
- gstack: 1.58.4.0 (`9fd03fa`), CLI built from same tree
- OS: macOS 26.3.1 (arm64), Bun 1.3.9
- Playwright: chromium_headless_shell-1208
- Target app: local Next.js 15 dev server (heavy routes, cold compiles routinely 15–60s)

## Symptom
During a `/browse`-driven QA session the headless daemon "randomly" dies and gets replaced 4 times in ~15 minutes. Every replacement loses all session state (login cookies, viewport, tabs), forcing a re-login each time. Nothing in the visible CLI output explains it — the daemon is spawned with `stdio: 'ignore'`, and the `[browse] Server connection lost. Restarting...` / `[browse] Starting server...` lines are easy to lose when output is filtered (e.g. `| tail -1`).

## Root cause (evidenced, reproduced 3× with daemon stderr captured)
The daemon never crashes on its own. The CLI kills it.

Chain, with refs at v1.58.4.0:

1. A `goto` to a heavy dev route exceeds the 15s navigation timeout. Playwright throws, the daemon **responds normally** (audit log: `goto … status=error durationMs=15010`) — but the navigation keeps loading in Chromium.
2. The next CLI command arrives while the daemon/Chromium is still churning (CDP console/network event flood from the dev app). The fetch fails at connection level, and `probeHealthWithBackoff` (`browse/src/cli.ts:251` — 3 attempts × 250ms backoff, 2s timeout each, so roughly a 7s grace window) also fails.
3. `sendCommand`'s catch (`browse/src/cli.ts:586-605`) declares the daemon dead: `killServer(oldState.pid)` → SIGTERM → daemon replaced → **all cookies/login lost** (ephemeral profile).
4. The daemon's death is then silent/garbled for two separate reasons filed separately: SIGTERM "ignore" being cosmetic (see companion issue) and the `browser?.process()` TypeError in `resolveDisconnectCause` (#2085, secondary problem — confirmed on macOS, comment there).

Captured daemon stderr at the moment of death (identical in all 3 reproductions):

```
[browse] Received SIGTERM (ignoring — use /stop or Ctrl+C for intentional shutdown)
[browse] Tab closed (id=1, remaining=0)
[browse] FATAL unhandled rejection: browser?.process is not a function. (In 'browser?.process()', 'browser?.process' is undefined)
```

Server-side audit log around one of the original deaths — note the daemon fully served the command, then was killed seconds later (next entry is the freshly spawned replacement):

```
15:11:11.135Z | viewport 1440x900 | ok  | dur: 27
15:11:26.233Z | goto /en/training | error | dur: 15010   <- daemon responded fine
15:11:37.532Z | screenshot        | ok  | dur: 34        <- answered by the REPLACEMENT daemon
```

## Why #1781's protections don't cover this
The #1781 fixes are present and working: the 30s AbortError path refuses to kill ("busy, not restarting"), and the connection-error path probes health before killing. But the probe window (~7s max) is shorter than how long a daemon stays unresponsive while Chromium loads a heavy dev-mode page with a timed-out navigation still in flight. So the "busy, not dead" case #1781 was designed to protect still ends in kill+restart — just via the connection-error branch instead of the AbortError branch.

Ruled out with evidence: kernel/jetsam kills (unified log clean), macOS crash reports (none), harness/session teardown (daemon is correctly detached: PPID 1, own pgid — and a manually session-isolated daemon died identically), `killOrphanChromium` (headed-profile lock only), terminal-agent watchdog (kills agents by identity record only), idle timeout.

## Repro (deterministic-ish, no special app needed)
1. Serve any route that takes >15s to finish loading (a cold-compiling Next.js dev route, or an endpoint that accepts and stalls).
2. Spawn the daemon with stderr captured: `BROWSE_PARENT_PID=0 bun run browse/src/server.ts 2>&1 | tee /tmp/browse.log`
3. `browse goto <slow-route>` → "Timeout 15000ms exceeded" (daemon healthy, nav still in flight).
4. Within ~5s, run any other browse command (`browse url`).
5. Observe: SIGTERM → tab closed → FATAL in the log; a fresh daemon answers the command; all session state gone.

Timing-dependent (needs the daemon to actually blow the ~7s probe window), so it fires reliably on heavy real pages and intermittently on synthetic ones. In my sessions it fired 4/4 times on cold dev routes.

## Suggested direction
- Lengthen/adapt the probe window when the last command was a navigation that timed out (the highest-probability "busy" state), or track "nav in flight" server-side and have `/health` report busy-but-alive so the CLI can distinguish.
- Consider reconnect-don't-respawn: if the PID is alive, keep waiting/retrying rather than killing — a lost session is strictly worse than a slow command for QA flows.
- On unavoidable restart, restore state automatically (the `state save/load` machinery already exists) so a kill doesn't cost the login.

title: Kiro/Codex/OpenCode setup doesn't copy supabase/config.sh — telemetry sync silently no-ops
author: jizusun
body:
## Bug

The `setup` script's Kiro install path (step 6) does not copy `supabase/config.sh` to `~/.kiro/skills/gstack/`. This means `gstack-telemetry-sync` can never resolve `GSTACK_SUPABASE_URL` and exits silently at:

```bash
[ -z "$SUPABASE_URL" ] && exit 0
```

Users who opt into `community` or `anonymous` telemetry on Kiro (and likely Codex/OpenCode) get no error — telemetry just never uploads.

## Root Cause

The Claude host path works because it symlinks the **entire repo root**, so `$GSTACK_DIR/supabase/config.sh` resolves naturally. The Kiro, Codex, and OpenCode paths only link selective assets (`bin/`, `browse/dist/`, `ETHOS.md`, etc.) and miss the `supabase/` directory.

In `gstack-telemetry-sync:31-34`:
```bash
if [ -z "${GSTACK_SUPABASE_URL:-}" ] && [ -f "$GSTACK_DIR/supabase/config.sh" ]; then
  . "$GSTACK_DIR/supabase/config.sh"
fi
SUPABASE_URL="${GSTACK_SUPABASE_URL:-}"
```

Since `$GSTACK_DIR` resolves to `~/.kiro/skills/gstack` (not the source repo), and `supabase/config.sh` was never copied there, `SUPABASE_URL` stays empty.

## Affected Hosts

- `--host kiro` (confirmed)
- `--host codex` (likely — `create_codex_runtime_root` also uses selective linking)
- `--host opencode` (likely — `create_opencode_runtime_root` same pattern)

## Fix

Add to each host's setup block:

```bash
mkdir -p "$TARGET_GSTACK/supabase"
_link_or_copy "$SOURCE_GSTACK_DIR/supabase/config.sh" "$TARGET_GSTACK/supabase/config.sh"
```

Where `$TARGET_GSTACK` is `$KIRO_GSTACK`, `$CODEX_GSTACK`, or `$OPENCODE_GSTACK` respectively.

## Environment

- gstack installed via `./setup --host kiro`
- Linux (Ubuntu)
- `~/.kiro/skills/gstack/supabase/` does not exist after setup
- Telemetry set to `community` — local JSONL writes work, remote sync silently skips

title: autoplan SKILL.md (1839 lines) is too large — split into router + references/ to save tokens
author: boyingliu01
body:
## Problem

The `autoplan` SKILL.md is **1839 lines** (~24,500 tokens at load time). This is **3.7x the recommended max of 500 lines** and triggers the `skill-cert` progressive disclosure check as a hard failure:

```
⚠️ Load tier too large: 24520t (limit: 5000t). Consider moving detailed instructions to references/.
⚠️ No references/ directory found. Skills over 2500t should use references/ for on-demand loading.
```

## Why this matters

Every time the model loads this skill, it pays ~24K tokens before executing a single instruction — that's 3-5x more than typical skills. Most of those tokens are consumed by the gstack shared preamble (lines 1-135), AskUserQuestion format reference (lines 325-447), and detailed phase-by-phase instruction blocks that the model only needs to reference on-demand during specific phases.

## Proposed pattern: Router + references/

The same pattern gstack already recommends for code organization (split large modules) should apply to skills. The router stays lean (~200-300 lines) — triggers, core decision principles, phase orchestration overview. Detailed instructions move to:

```
gstack-autoplan/
├── SKILL.md                          # ~300 lines: router only
├── references/
│   ├── preamble.md                   # Shared gstack preamble (already reused across all skills!)
│   ├── askuserquestion-format.md     # AskUserQuestion spec (already reused across all skills!)
│   ├── ceo-review.md                 # Phase 1: full CEO review instructions
│   ├── design-review.md              # Phase 2: design review instructions
│   ├── eng-review.md                 # Phase 3: eng review instructions
│   ├── dx-review.md                  # Phase 3.5: DX review instructions
│   ├── final-gate.md                 # Phase 4: approval gate
│   └── decision-audit-trail.md       # Audit trail format
```

The router reads references/ on demand as each phase starts. Skill-level context is preserved exactly — nothing is removed, just lazy-loaded.

## Token savings estimate

- **Current**: ~24,500 tokens at load
- **After refactor**: ~6,000 tokens at load (router only), ~18,500 tokens loaded on-demand
- **Net savings**: ~18,500 tokens per load when not using autoplan, equivalent savings for other preamble-sharing skills

## Note on shared preamble

The preamble (lines 1-135) is currently duplicated across ALL gstack skills. This is another source of waste. If the preamble were extracted to a shared `references/preamble.md` loaded once per session, every skill invocation saves those ~2,000 tokens. This is a larger refactor but worth considering.

title: gstack-repo-mode bypasses gstack-slug's cache (state written under a divergent slug) and skips slug sanitization before mkdir
author: georgemsiii
body:
**Version:** gstack v1.58.5.0 (macOS)

`bin/gstack-repo-mode` derives its own project slug instead of consuming `bin/gstack-slug`, which causes two related problems: project state written under a divergent slug (with a silent-history-loss failure mode), and a slug that skips the `[a-zA-Z0-9._-]` sanitization contract before being used in a `mkdir` path.

## Bug 1: `gstack-repo-mode` bypasses `gstack-slug`'s cache → state written under a divergent slug

`gstack-slug` is cache-first by design — the comment on its cache-first read says the per-PWD cache (`~/.gstack/slug-cache/<path-key>`) "guarantees consistency across sessions". Whatever slug was derived first (e.g. a basename slug cached before the repo had a remote) stays canonical, and all project state accumulates under `~/.gstack/projects/<cached-slug>/`.

But `gstack-repo-mode` ([lines 16–21](https://github.com/garrytan/gstack/blob/11de390be1be6849eb9a15f91ff4922dd16c589a/bin/gstack-repo-mode#L16-L21)) deliberately re-derives the slug from `git remote get-url origin` and never consults that cache:

```bash
# Compute SLUG directly (avoid eval of gstack-slug — branch names can contain shell metacharacters)
REMOTE_URL=$(git remote get-url origin 2>/dev/null || true)
...
SLUG=$(echo "$REMOTE_URL" | sed 's|.*[:/]\([^/]*/[^/]*\)\.git$|\1|;s|.*[:/]\([^/]*/[^/]*\)$|\1|' | tr '/' '-')
```

So in any repo whose **cached** canonical slug differs from the remote-derived form, every repo-mode classification writes `repo-mode.json` into a **twin directory** `~/.gstack/projects/<owner-repo>/`, while timeline/decisions/checkpoints live under `~/.gstack/projects/<cached-slug>/`.

### Why this is worse than clutter

If the slug-cache entry is ever lost (cache dir cleaned, path moved, new machine), `gstack-slug` re-derives the remote-form slug and resolution **flips to the twin**. Because the twin is non-empty (it holds `repo-mode.json`), anything that treats "non-empty project dir" as "initialized" sees a healthy project — and context restore silently returns a near-empty history instead of months of accumulated state. No error, no signal.

### Repro

1. `mkdir myproj && cd myproj && git init` (no remote yet)
2. Run `gstack-slug` → caches slug `myproj` (basename fallback) in `~/.gstack/slug-cache/`
3. Use any gstack feature that writes project state (context save, timeline, decision log) → real history accumulates under `~/.gstack/projects/myproj/`
4. `git remote add origin git@github.com:me/myproj.git`, make ≥5 commits
5. From the repo root, run `gstack-repo-mode` → creates `~/.gstack/projects/me-myproj/repo-mode.json`
6. `ls ~/.gstack/projects/` → both `myproj/` (canonical, holds the real history) and `me-myproj/` (twin)

Observed in the wild: 4 twin dirs on one machine (each containing a lone `repo-mode.json`), all in repos whose slug was cached before the remote existed.

### Suggested fix

Parse the `SLUG=` line from `gstack-slug` output instead of re-deriving — sed, **not** eval, which respects the exact injection concern in repo-mode's own comment:

```bash
SLUG=$("$SCRIPT_DIR/gstack-slug" | sed -n 's/^SLUG=//p' | head -1)
```

`gstack-slug` sanitizes its output to `[a-zA-Z0-9._-]` on every path before echoing, so consuming the line as data (never eval'd) is safe, and repo-mode inherits the cached canonical slug. Two details matter for the swap:

- **Keep the existing no-remote early exit** (`REPO_MODE=unknown` when `git remote get-url origin` is empty) *before* this line. `gstack-slug` falls back to the directory basename when there's no remote, so calling it unconditionally would make local-only repos eligible for classification they're excluded from today.
- **Invoke from the repo root.** `gstack-slug`'s cache is keyed on `$PWD`, so a subdirectory invocation reads/writes a different cache entry than the repo root's. Something like `SLUG=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)" && "$SCRIPT_DIR/gstack-slug" | sed -n 's/^SLUG=//p' | head -1)`.

Existing stray `repo-mode.json` twins can be deleted or ignored — repo-mode recomputes its cache on the next classification either way.

## Bug 2: the re-derived slug skips sanitization before `mkdir ~/.gstack/projects/$SLUG`

`gstack-slug` enforces a `[a-zA-Z0-9._-]` output contract (`tr -cd 'a-zA-Z0-9._-'` on every path, including a final unconditional re-sanitize). `gstack-repo-mode`'s derivation only converts `/` → `-` (line 21) — no character filtering — and then uses the value in a filesystem path:

```bash
CACHE_DIR="$HOME/.gstack/projects/$SLUG"   # line 37
...
mkdir -p "$CACHE_DIR" 2>/dev/null || true  # line 87
```

A git remote URL is free-form local config (`git remote add origin` accepts any string — typos, templates, odd hosting URLs), so unusual URLs produce directory names containing spaces, `~`, `$`, quotes, or control characters — violating the `[a-zA-Z0-9._-]` contract that `gstack-slug` enforces when it creates entries in the same tree. To be precise about blast radius: `$SLUG` is quoted at every use and only the `REPO_MODE=` line is sourced by consumers, and the slash-to-dash conversion means no path separator survives into the name — so this is a filesystem-invariant violation (surprise directory names under `~/.gstack/projects/`), not shell injection or path traversal.

The Bug 1 fix subsumes this one: consuming `gstack-slug`'s sanitized output removes the unsanitized derivation entirely. A minimal standalone fix would be appending `| tr -cd 'a-zA-Z0-9._-'` to [line 21](https://github.com/garrytan/gstack/blob/11de390be1be6849eb9a15f91ff4922dd16c589a/bin/gstack-repo-mode#L21).

title: gstack-config key validator rejects brain_trust_policy@local (endpoint-hash returns literal 'local' for local engines)
author: mmakunas
body:
## What happened

`/setup-gbrain` Step 9.5 persists the brain trust policy with:

```bash
~/.claude/skills/gstack/bin/gstack-config set brain_trust_policy@$_HASH personal
```

where `_HASH=$(gstack-config endpoint-hash)`. For local engines (PGLite path), `endpoint-hash` returns the literal string `local`, but gstack-config's key validator only accepts hex suffixes after `@` (`@[a-f0-9]+`), so the `set` call is rejected and the policy is never persisted under the per-endpoint key.

## Impact

- Step 9.5 fails on every fresh local-PGLite setup (the default try-first path).
- On subsequent runs, `gstack-config get brain_trust_policy@local` returns unset, so skills see the policy as never-set and retry the auto-set each time (harmless but noisy, and the per-endpoint tracking the feature was built for never engages for local engines).
- `/sync-gbrain` Step 1's trust-policy gate hits the same mismatch.

## Workaround

Store under the plain key: `gstack-config set brain_trust_policy personal`. Works, but loses the per-endpoint scoping.

## Suggested fix (either works)

1. Allow `local` as a valid endpoint-hash value in the key validator (`@([a-f0-9]+|local)`), or
2. Have `endpoint-hash` return a stable hex digest for local engines instead of the literal `local`.

## Environment

- gstack 1.58.5.0, macOS (Darwin 25.5.0)
- gbrain 0.42.26.0, engine=pglite, mode=local-stdio
- Repro: fresh `/setup-gbrain` picking Path 3 (PGLite local), reach Step 9.5

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: Conductor + Claude: plan-tune PreToolUse hook breaks native AskUserQuestion (auto-installed in Conductor)
author: philhie
body:
## Summary

In **Conductor** sessions running **Claude Code**, gstack's plan-tune AUQ hooks **break Conductor's native `AskUserQuestion`**. Every AUQ call returns `[Tool result missing due to internal error]` (or the input is echoed with no rendered options). The same Conductor workspace running **Codex/GPT works fine**, because Codex doesn't load `~/.claude/settings.json` hooks.

The important part: the premise behind the v1.58.1.0 "Conductor prose AskUserQuestion" feature — *"Conductor disables native AUQ and its MCP variant is flaky"* — appears to be **self-inflicted**. Conductor's AUQ is not flaky. gstack's own PreToolUse hook is what breaks it, and then the code attributes the failure to Conductor and routes to prose.

## Impact

- Claude + Conductor users lose clickable `AskUserQuestion` entirely (every decision silently degrades to prose, or errors).
- It's auto-installed with no opt-in: `setup` treats a Conductor workspace as an implicit opt-in for the plan-tune hooks (see below), so a routine `/gstack-upgrade` silently plants the breakage.

## Root cause

`AskUserQuestion` in Conductor + Claude Code is resolved through the Agent-SDK `canUseTool` elicitation bridge (`CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRYPOINT=sdk-ts`; the `mcp__conductor__AskUserQuestion` tool is injected by Conductor's harness, not present in `~/.claude.json`).

gstack registers a **PreToolUse hook** (`hosts/claude/hooks/question-preference-hook.ts`, source `plan-tune-cathedral`) on the matcher `(AskUserQuestion|mcp__.*__AskUserQuestion)`. When that hook participates in the permission flow — **even when it returns `permissionDecision: "defer"`** — it corrupts the `canUseTool` round-trip, and the tool result comes back missing.

`setup` auto-installs it in Conductor:

```sh
# setup, ~line 1389
if [ "$PT_DECISION" = "prompt" ] && { [ -n "${CONDUCTOR_WORKSPACE_PATH:-}" ] || [ -n "${CONDUCTOR_PORT:-}" ]; }; then
  PT_DECISION="yes"
  _PT_CONDUCTOR_AUTO=1
fi
```

Introduced in **v1.58.1.0** (`c7ae632`, "feat: hermetic local E2E + Conductor prose AskUserQuestion").

## Reproduction

1. Open a Conductor workspace, Claude Code agent, with gstack's plan-tune hooks installed (the Conductor default).
2. Trigger any `AskUserQuestion` / `mcp__conductor__AskUserQuestion` call.
3. Observe: no options render; result is `[Tool result missing due to internal error]`.
4. Remove the AUQ hooks from `~/.claude/settings.json` (or `gstack-config set plan_tune_hooks no`, delete the `hooks` block, restart).
5. Trigger AUQ again → it renders and works. (Verified live, 3×.)

Cross-check: the identical Conductor workspace on Codex/GPT renders AUQ fine throughout — Codex never reads `~/.claude/settings.json`, so the hook is never in the path. This isolates the hook as the cause.

Note: gating the `deny` behind a flag so the hook returns `defer` does **not** fix it — the hook has to be fully **unregistered**. Its mere presence in the permission flow is enough to break the bridge.

## Suggested fix

At minimum, stop auto-installing the AUQ **PreToolUse** hook in Conductor, since it's what breaks native AUQ there:

```diff
-  # Conductor host reliability: ... Treat Conductor as an implicit opt-in ...
-  if [ "$PT_DECISION" = "prompt" ] && { [ -n "${CONDUCTOR_WORKSPACE_PATH:-}" ] || [ -n "${CONDUCTOR_PORT:-}" ]; }; then
-    PT_DECISION="yes"
-    _PT_CONDUCTOR_AUTO=1
-  fi
```

More fundamentally, it's worth re-validating the whole "Conductor prose AUQ" premise: native AUQ works in Conductor when this PreToolUse hook isn't registered, so the flakiness the feature works around looks like it's caused by the feature itself. If the PostToolUse capture/fallback hooks are confirmed harmless to the bridge (they fire after the result, so they likely are), the preference-enforcement value could be kept by only dropping the PreToolUse participation in Conductor — but that needs a test on real Conductor hardware.

## Workaround (for affected users)

```sh
# unregister the AUQ hooks and stop setup from re-adding them (even in Conductor)
gstack-config set plan_tune_hooks no
# then remove the plan-tune-cathedral / auq-error-fallback hooks from ~/.claude/settings.json
```

## Environment

- gstack `v1.58.5.0`
- Claude Code `2.1.201` via Agent SDK (`CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRYPOINT=sdk-ts`)
- Conductor (macOS, local mode)
- Regression introduced in gstack `v1.58.1.0` (`c7ae632`)

Happy to open a PR from a fork if the minimal `setup` change above looks right to you.

title: plan-tune question-log hook records answers as `__unknown__` on native AskUserQuestion; `followed_recommendation` always false
author: christhefarmer

title: setup: cleanup_old_claude_symlinks() derives orphans from the payload dir, so it can never clean orphans (37 dead skill dirs, ~3 months)
author: UltraIntelligence
body:
## Summary

`cleanup_old_claude_symlinks()` discovers orphaned skill directories by iterating the gstack payload directory. When the payload is gone — which is exactly when orphans exist — the loop body never executes, so the orphans are never cleaned. The function is structurally incapable of running in the situation it exists to handle.

In my case this left **37 dead skill directories** in a project repo for roughly three months. Each `.claude/skills/<name>/` contained only a `SKILL.md` symlink into a `.claude/skills/gstack/` payload that no longer existed. They never loaded; nothing surfaced an error; `/gstack-upgrade` never repaired them.

Verified present on `origin/main` @ `11de390` (v1.58.5.0).

## The defect

`setup:613` (as of `11de390`):

```bash
cleanup_old_claude_symlinks() {
  local gstack_dir="$1"
  local skills_dir="$2"
  local removed=()
  for skill_dir in "$gstack_dir"/*/; do        # <-- iterates the PAYLOAD
    if [ -f "$skill_dir/SKILL.md" ]; then
      ...
      elif [ -d "$old_target" ] && [ -L "$old_target/SKILL.md" ]; then
        link_dest="$(readlink "$old_target/SKILL.md" 2>/dev/null || true)"
        case "$link_dest" in
          *gstack*) rm -rf "$old_target"; removed+=("$skill_name") ;;
```

The list of directories to clean is derived from `"$gstack_dir"/*/`. If `$gstack_dir` has been deleted, the glob doesn't match, the loop body never runs, and zero orphans are removed. The orphans live in `$skills_dir`, not `$gstack_dir` — that's where the scan should happen.

## Why `/gstack-upgrade` can't recover

`gstack-upgrade/SKILL.md`, install-type detection:

```bash
elif [ -d ".claude/skills/gstack" ]; then       INSTALL_TYPE="vendored"
elif [ -d "$HOME/.claude/skills/gstack" ]; then INSTALL_TYPE="vendored-global"
```

Once the project payload is deleted, detection falls through to `vendored-global`. Every subsequent upgrade operates on `$HOME/.claude/skills/gstack` and never revisits the project directory. The orphans are permanent — I had to remove them by hand.

## How the payload likely disappeared

The team-mode branch in `gstack-upgrade/SKILL.md` deletes the payload and does not touch its 37 siblings:

```bash
git rm -r --cached .claude/skills/gstack/ 2>/dev/null || true
if ! grep -qF '.claude/skills/gstack/' .gitignore 2>/dev/null; then
  echo '.claude/skills/gstack/' >> .gitignore
fi
rm -rf "$LOCAL_GSTACK"
```

My repo's `.gitignore` contains exactly that appended line, committed the same day the 37 symlinks were created. I want to be careful here: I **cannot prove** this branch ran — `gstack-config get team_mode` returns empty on my machine today, and the `.gitignore` line landed inside a human commit, so it may have been added by hand.

It also doesn't matter much, because the payload is gitignored in the vendored layout, so **any** of these removes it with no way back:

- `git clean -fdx`
- a fresh clone or `git worktree add`
- the team-mode branch above

Whatever the trigger, `cleanup_old_claude_symlinks()` cannot subsequently clean up. That's the part worth fixing.

## Reproduction

```bash
# from a repo with a vendored gstack install
rm -rf .claude/skills/gstack          # simulate clean -fdx / fresh worktree / team-mode branch
~/.claude/skills/gstack/setup         # or: /gstack-upgrade

# every .claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md is now a dangling symlink, and stays that way
find .claude/skills -maxdepth 2 -name SKILL.md -type l ! -exec test -e {} \; -print
```

These orphans are easy to miss: a directory whose `SKILL.md` is a dangling symlink is invisible to `[ -f "$d/SKILL.md" ]` but still shows up in `find` and in a plain `ls`. `diff -rq` against a good copy reports `DIFFERS`, which reads as "contents differ" and sent me down the wrong path for a while.

## Suggested fix

Scan the destination, not the source. Roughly:

```bash
cleanup_old_claude_symlinks() {
  local gstack_dir="$1" skills_dir="$2"
  local removed=()
  for old_target in "$skills_dir"/*/; do
    [ -L "$old_target/SKILL.md" ] || continue
    link_dest="$(readlink "$old_target/SKILL.md" 2>/dev/null || true)"
    case "$link_dest" in *gstack*) ;; *) continue ;; esac
    # dangling, or points into the payload we're replacing → stale either way
    if [ ! -e "$old_target/SKILL.md" ] || [ -n "$gstack_dir" ]; then
      rm -rf "$old_target"
      removed+=("$(basename "$old_target")")
    fi
  done
  [ ${#removed[@]} -gt 0 ] && echo "  cleaned up old entries: ${removed[*]}"
}
```

This keeps the existing migration behavior (payload present → clean the flat names it's replacing) and additionally reaps dangling `*gstack*` symlinks when the payload is gone. The `*gstack*` guard on `readlink` preserves the current provenance check, so user-authored skills are never touched.

Two smaller things that would have helped independently:

1. **`setup` could warn** when it finds `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` symlinks with no payload, even if it declines to delete them.
2. **The team-mode branch** in `gstack-upgrade/SKILL.md` should call the cleanup helper after `rm -rf "$LOCAL_GSTACK"`, rather than leaving the siblings behind.

## Environment

- gstack `1.58.5.0` (`11de390`), global install at `~/.claude/skills/gstack`
- macOS (Darwin 27.0.0), Claude Code `2.1.205`
- Affected project had a vendored install created 2026-04-12; orphans discovered and removed 2026-07-09

## Possibly related

- #954 — `setup` creates absolute symlinks for vendored installs, breaks portability
- #896 (closed) — `gstack-uninstall` leaves orphaned `~/.claude/skills/gstack-*` directories (prefix-mode)
- #1882 — skills hardcode `~/.claude/skills/gstack/` paths

Distinct from all three: this is the *flat-name* layout, the payload is absent rather than misnamed or non-portable, and the failure is in cleanup reachability rather than path resolution.

title: Install footprint: 1.1GB in ~/.claude/skills (739MB node_modules + 21MB .git) and connect-chrome duplicates open-gstack-browser
author: maia-rafael-br
body:
Two packaging observations from a routine setup audit of a gstack install (v1.58.x):

**1. Install size.** The gstack skill directory is a full git clone weighing ~1.1GB inside `~/.claude/skills/gstack`: 739MB `node_modules`, 151MB `browse`, ~61MB each for `design`/`bin`/`make-pdf`, plus a 21MB `.git`. For a skills directory that agents scan every session, a slimmer release artifact (pruned dev dependencies, shallow/no .git, optional heavy modules downloaded on first use) would help.

**2. Duplicate skill registration.** `connect-chrome/` ships byte-identical to `open-gstack-browser/`, so both register as separate skills with the same content. Deleting one locally works until `/gstack-upgrade` restores it — probably worth deduplicating at the packaging level (alias or single skill).

title: connect-chrome and open-gstack-browser install with the same frontmatter name, so one silently shadows the other
author: notjbg
body:
## Summary

`connect-chrome` and `open-gstack-browser` are installed into `~/.claude/skills/` as two separate skill directories that both declare `name: open-gstack-browser` in their frontmatter. Claude Code keys skills on that frontmatter name, so the two collide: one is silently discarded, and **which one survives follows unsorted `readdir` order**.

On my machine `connect-chrome` won, so `/open-gstack-browser` is not a registered command — even though `README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, the router `SKILL.md`, and the sidebar extension's copy-to-clipboard button all tell users to run exactly that.

## Why it happens

In the repo, `connect-chrome` is a symlink to `open-gstack-browser` (one skill, correct). The collision is created at install time, by two independent paths:

1. **`bin/gstack-relink`** discovers skills with `for skill_dir in "$INSTALL_DIR"/*/`. The trailing-slash glob matches the `connect-chrome` symlink, and the loop names the installed entry from the *directory basename* while symlinking the canonical `SKILL.md` into it. Result: `skills/connect-chrome/SKILL.md -> .../open-gstack-browser/SKILL.md`, carrying `name: open-gstack-browser`.

2. **`setup`** then re-creates the same alias explicitly:
   ```bash
   # Backwards-compat alias: /connect-chrome → /open-gstack-browser
   _OGB_LINK="$INSTALL_SKILLS_DIR/connect-chrome"
   ...
   _link_or_copy "gstack/open-gstack-browser" "$_OGB_LINK"
   ```
   which symlinks the alias directory straight at the canonical skill directory — so its `SKILL.md` is, again, the canonical one.

Note that `setup`'s own `link_claude_skill_dirs` is *not* the culprit: it derives the entry name from the frontmatter `name:`, so it maps both directories onto a single `open-gstack-browser` target. Only the two paths above mint a second, differently-named directory holding the same frontmatter name.

## Reproduction

Against `11de390` (v1.58.5.0), using only the shipped scripts:

```bash
T=$(mktemp -d); I="$T/gstack-install"; S="$T/skills"
mkdir -p "$I/bin" "$S"
cp bin/gstack-config bin/gstack-relink bin/gstack-patch-names "$I/bin/"; chmod +x "$I/bin"/*
mkdir -p "$I/open-gstack-browser"
printf -- '---\nname: open-gstack-browser\ndescription: launch\n---\n# ogb\n' > "$I/open-gstack-browser/SKILL.md"
ln -s open-gstack-browser "$I/connect-chrome"          # exactly as the repo ships it
printf -- '---\nname: gstack\ndescription: root\n---\n# root\n' > "$I/SKILL.md"

GSTACK_STATE_DIR="$T" GSTACK_INSTALL_DIR="$I" GSTACK_SKILLS_DIR="$S" "$I/bin/gstack-config" set skill_prefix false
GSTACK_STATE_DIR="$T" GSTACK_INSTALL_DIR="$I" GSTACK_SKILLS_DIR="$S" "$I/bin/gstack-relink"

for d in "$S"/*/; do
  echo "$(basename "$d") -> $(grep -m1 '^name:' "$d/SKILL.md")"
done
```

Actual:

```
_gstack-command -> name: gstack
connect-chrome -> name: open-gstack-browser      # <-- collides
open-gstack-browser -> name: open-gstack-browser # <-- collides
```

Expected: three distinct names.

This also matches a real install (`~/.claude/skills/connect-chrome/SKILL.md` is a symlink into `gstack/connect-chrome/SKILL.md`).

## Impact

- `/open-gstack-browser` is missing on an arbitrary subset of installs, while every doc and the browser extension reference it.
- Which command exists is non-deterministic across machines, so the failure is hard to reproduce and reads to users as "gstack is broken on my laptop."
- A user auditing their skills sees two entries for one skill.

## Suggested fix

Install the backwards-compat alias as a *thin skill with its own `name:`* that delegates to the canonical skill, rather than as a symlink to the canonical skill directory. Then both `/connect-chrome` and `/open-gstack-browser` register, on every machine.

I have a PR ready that does this in `bin/gstack-relink` (making it the single owner of the alias), removes the now-duplicated alias blocks from `setup`, and adds five regression tests to `test/relink.test.ts` covering the name-uniqueness invariant, the `skill_prefix` flip, idempotency, and in-place upgrade of existing symlinked aliases.

## Environment

- gstack `11de390` (v1.58.5.0)
- Claude Code 2.1.205, native install, macOS 15 (Darwin 25.5.0)
- bun 1.3.14

title: Install footprint: 1.1GB in ~/.claude/skills (739MB node_modules + 21MB .git) and connect-chrome duplicates open-gstack-browser
author: maia-rafael-br
body:
Two packaging observations from a routine setup audit of a gstack install (v1.58.x):

**1. Install size.** The gstack skill directory is a full git clone weighing ~1.1GB inside `~/.claude/skills/gstack`: 739MB `node_modules`, 151MB `browse`, ~61MB each for `design`/`bin`/`make-pdf`, plus a 21MB `.git`. For a skills directory that agents scan every session, a slimmer release artifact (pruned dev dependencies, shallow/no .git, optional heavy modules downloaded on first use) would help.

**2. Duplicate skill registration.** `connect-chrome/` ships byte-identical to `open-gstack-browser/`, so both register as separate skills with the same content. Deleting one locally works until `/gstack-upgrade` restores it — probably worth deduplicating at the packaging level (alias or single skill).
